title: Level 5: Timers (Timer0 Normal Mode)
aim: To use Timer 0 in Normal Mode and the Overflow Interrupt on the ATmega328P to blink an LED without blocking the main program.
objective: Understand how 8-bit timer registers (TCCR0B, TIMSK0) work, calculate prescaler and timer overflows, and use ISR(TIMER0_OVF_vect) for precise timing.
theory:
Hardware Timers

Instead of software delay loops (like _delay_ms), the ATmega328P contains dedicated hardware counters called Timers. Timer0 is an 8-bit timer, meaning it repeatedly counts from 0 up to 255. Once it hits 255 and tries to go to 256, it overflows back to 0.

The Prescaler

Running at 16 MHz, a timer would overflow in just 16 microseconds! A Prescaler slows down the clock feeding the timer by dividing it. If we set a prescaler of 1024 (using the TCCR0B register), the timer ticks at 16,000,000 / 1024 = 15,625 Hz. One overflow then takes roughly 16.38 milliseconds.

Timer Interrupts

When the timer overflows, it sets a flag (TOV0). By configuring the TIMSK0 (Timer Interrupt Mask Register) to enable the Timer Overflow Interrupt, the CPU immediately calls the Overflow ISR (Interrupt Service Routine). This frees up your main loop to handle things like reading buttons simultaneously, because the blinking is handled asynchronously in the background.

pretest.question: Which of these registers controls the prescaler for Timer0?
pretest.explanation: TCCR0B (Timer/Counter Control Register 0 B) handles setting the prescaler and starting the timer clock.

pretest.question: If Timer0 is an 8-bit counter, at what value does it naturally overflow when running in Normal Mode?
pretest.explanation: The counter holds 8 bits (0-255). It overflows when trying to step past 255.

pretest.question: What is the primary purpose of the DDRx register in the ATmega328P?
options: To configure pins as input or output
pretest.explanation: DDR configures the direction of each GPIO pin.

pretest.question: Which header file provides basic I/O register definitions for AVR?
pretest.explanation: <avr/io.h> provides register definitions for all AVR peripherals.

pretest.question: What does the volatile keyword tell the C compiler?
options: May change unexpectedly, so do not optimize reads
pretest.explanation: volatile prevents the compiler from caching the variable.

pretest.question: How many GPIO pins does the ATmega328P have in total?
pretest.explanation: 23 GPIO pins across Port B, C, and D.

pretest.question: What is the maximum clock frequency of the ATmega328P?
pretest.explanation: Supports up to 20MHz, though Arduino UNO uses 16MHz.

pretest.question: What is the purpose of sei()?
pretest.explanation: sei() sets the global interrupt enable flag in SREG.

pretest.question: Which AVR memory type retains data after power-off?
What is the width of each ATmega328P port register?
pretest.explanation: The ATmega328P is an 8-bit MCU; all port registers are 8 bits wide.

procedure: Set the DDR for an LED pin (e.g., PB5) connected to Pin 13 to act as an output.
Configure TCCR0B to set the prescaler for Timer0 to 1024 by setting CS02 and CS00 to 1.
Enable the Timer0 Overflow Interrupt by setting TOIE0 to 1 in the TIMSK0 register.
Turn on global interrupts using sei();.
Inside the ISR, setup a static counter variable. Increment it every overflow.
Since one overflow is ~16ms, toggle the LED state when the static counter reaches 61 (1000ms / 16.38ms). Reset the counter to 0.
Leave the main loop (while(1)) completely empty. The background hardware handles everything!

posttest.question: If we leave the main while(1) loop completely empty, what is the CPU doing?
options: Nothing, it goes to sleep automatically
Endlessly spinning in the while loop until the background interrupt disrupts it
posttest.explanation: The CPU endlessly spins in the empty loop, continuously checking if an interrupt has been flagged by the hardware module.

posttest.question: Why is it good practice to use #define for pin numbers?
posttest.explanation: #define creates named constants for self-documenting code.

posttest.question: What tool converts C source to .hex for AVR?
posttest.explanation: avr-gcc compiles C to AVR machine code.

posttest.question: What is the difference between PORTB and PINB?
options: PORTB writes output; PINB reads actual pin voltages
posttest.explanation: PORTB drives outputs; PINB reads real-time pin states.

posttest.question: What happens to SRAM when power is removed?
What is the stack used for in AVR?
posttest.explanation: Stack stores return addresses and local variables.

posttest.question: Which is NOT a valid ATmega328P port?
posttest.explanation: ATmega328P has only Port B, C, and D. No Port A.

posttest.question: What is a watchdog timer used for?
posttest.explanation: WDT auto-resets MCU when not periodically fed.

posttest.question: What does the SREG register contain?
posttest.explanation: SREG holds CPU flags and the global interrupt enable bit.

posttest.question: In an ISR, interrupts are automatically:
posttest.explanation: AVR clears I-flag in SREG upon entering an ISR.

feedback: Perfect! You successfully broke free from blocking delays by harnessing autonomous hardware timers. This is crucial for real-time operating systems.
